188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.

108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

39	## Decisions identified
40	
41	### Decision 1: how to express the generic rank `m`
42	
43	- **Mathlib idiom**: `m := Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d) (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N)`.
44	  `P_d = MvPolynomial (Fin d) A` is a noetherian domain (`MvPolynomial.isDomain`, Hilbert basis), so
45	  `nonZeroDivisors P_d` localizes `N` to a module over `Localization (nonZeroDivisors P_d) = FractionRing P_d`,
46	  a finite-dimensional `FractionRing P_d`-vector space (finite, since `N` is finite over `P_d`).
47	  `Module.finrank` over a field is the right invariant; `Module.rank` (`Cardinal`-valued) is the wrong
48	  altitude — you want a `ℕ` to index `Fin m`.
49	- **Choosing `m` lifts of `N` whose images form a basis** — atoms all verified:
50	  - `Module.finBasis (FractionRing P_d) (LocalizedModule … N) : Basis (Fin m) (FractionRing P_d) (…)`
51	    (`Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.Free`) — the field-vector-space basis indexed by `Fin (finrank)`.
52	  - `IsLocalizedModule.surj (nonZeroDivisors P_d) (LocalizedModule.mkLinearMap …)` — every basis vector
53	    is `(1/s) • (image of some n ∈ N)`; `s` a unit in the field, so the **images** `{mk n_i}` are again a
54	    basis (clear denominators). (`Mathlib.Algebra.Module.LocalizedModule.Basic`.)
55	  - `LinearIndependent.restrict_scalars` (P_d ↪ FractionRing P_d injective) descends linear independence
56	    of the lifts from `FractionRing P_d` back to `P_d` — this is what makes `φ` (below) injective.
57	  - Reverse helper `Module.Basis.ofIsLocalizedModule` / `LinearIndependent.localization`
58	    (`Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Module`) go R→Rₛ; useful for the spanning/surjectivity half.
59	- **Gap**: divergent-equivalent — Mathlib has every atom but **no single packaged "choose `m` elements
60	  whose images are a basis of the localization" lemma**. The number is idiomatic; the lift is project glue.
61	- **Verdict**: ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB on the number (`Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d) (LocalizedModule …)`),
62	  NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL on the lift (small, all anchors present).
63	
64	### Decision 2: encoding the generic-rank SES `gf_generic_rank_ses`
65	
66	- **Mathlib idiom for the map**: `φ := Fintype.linearCombination P_d v` where `v : Fin m → N` are the
67	  lifts of Decision 1 (`Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Finsupp.LinearCombination`). Domain `(Fin m → P_d)` **is**
68	  the concrete `P_d^{⊕m}` (matches the `M'` slot of L3 exactly). Companions: `Fintype.linearCombination_apply`
69	  (`∑ i, f i • v i`), `Fintype.range_linearCombination` (`range = span (Set.range v)`).
70	  `φ` injective ⟺ `LinearIndependent P_d v` (got from Decision 1 via `restrict_scalars`).
71	- **Cokernel / torsion**: `T := N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ`; the surjection is `Submodule.mkQ (range φ)`
72	  (surjective, and `Function.Exact φ (mkQ (range φ))` holds — `range φ = ker (mkQ)`). `T` torsion encoded
73	  as `Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T` (preferred — it is the literal input of the reindex's
74	  annihilator anchor) or equivalently `Subsingleton (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) T)` via
75	  `LocalizedModule.subsingleton_iff`. Torsion holds because the images of `v` span the localization
76	  (basis), so `φ ⊗ FractionRing P_d` is surjective, hence `T ⊗ FractionRing P_d = 0`.
77	- **Important simplification vs the directive**: the SES is constructible over `P_d` **directly — no
78	  inversion of `g ∈ A` is needed for this step**. `φ` is injective on the nose (`ker φ` is torsion in the
79	  torsion-free `P_d`-module `P_d^{⊕m}`, hence `0`), and `T` is torsion on the nose. The `g` that Nitsure
80	  mentions belongs entirely to the *reindex* (Decision 3), not the SES. So `gf_generic_rank_ses` carries
81	  no `g`. (The "denominator-clearing exactness after inverting one element" API the directive asked about
82	  is therefore unnecessary here.)
83	- **Gap**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL — no packaged generic-rank SES, but every atom exists and the assembly
84	  is short.
85	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (build `gf_generic_rank_ses` from the atoms above).

87	### Decision 3: the torsion reindex `gf_torsion_reindex` — and the base-domain induction shape
88	
89	- **Nonzero annihilator (Mathlib ✓)**: `Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors`
90	  (`Mathlib.Algebra.Module.Torsion.Basic`): a finite `Module.IsTorsion R M` over comm `R` has
91	  `(⊤.annihilator ∩ nonZeroDivisors R).Nonempty`. For the domain `P_d` this gives `0 ≠ F ∈ Ann_{P_d}(T)`.
92	- **Variable drop (Mathlib-ABSENT)**: from `0 ≠ F ∈ Ann(T)`, one wants `T` module-finite over a polynomial
93	  ring in `m' < d` variables. Cleanest route that **avoids Krull-dimension theory**: a Nagata change of
94	  variables makes `F` monic in `X_d` up to its leading coefficient `g ∈ A`; after inverting `g`,
95	  `A_g[X_1..X_d]/(F)` is module-finite (division algorithm) over `A_g[X_1..X_{d-1}] = MvPolynomial (Fin (d-1)) A_g`,
96	  and `T` (finite over `P_d/(F)`) is finite over it by `Module.Finite.trans`. So `m' = d-1 < d` — **no

98	  (`Mathlib.RingTheory.NoetherNormalization`) and `ringKrullDim` theory (`Mathlib.RingTheory.KrullDimension.*`,

102	- **CRITICAL design consequence — the induction must generalize the base domain `A`.** The reindex inverts
103	  `g ∈ A`, so `T_g` is finite over `MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g)` — base ring `A_g`, **not**
104	  `A`. The IH of `exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial` as currently written fixes `A`, so
105	  `IH m' _ T_g` does **not typecheck** (wrong base ring). This is the real cause of the iter-006

108	  domain — `∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) […], ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Free A_f N_f`,

112	  base `A_g` (still a noetherian domain: `IsLocalization.isDomain`/`Localization.Away` of a noeth domain).
113	- **Descent of the IH witness back to `A`**: IH gives `h ≠ 0` in `A_g` with `T_g` free over `(A_g)_h`.
114	  Since `A_g = Localization.Away g` and `h = a / g^k`, `(A_g)_h ≅ A_{g·a}` and `T_{g·a} ≅ (T_g)_h`, so the
115	  freeness descends to `f'' := g·a ∈ A`. This is the same localization-tower freeness transport as the
116	  already-proved L3b `free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul` (a thin bridge across `A → A_g → (A_g)_h`).

119	  `A_{f'}` for *any* `f' ≠ 0`. (Note: the file's comment "applied coordinatewise via the d=0 leaf" is

121	- **Gap**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (variable-drop engine + base-`A` generalization).
122	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL.

126	```lean

128	theorem gf_generic_rank_ses
129	    (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
130	    (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
131	    [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
132	    [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :

143	`exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial`. -/

145	    (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
146	    (d : ℕ) (hd : 0 < d) (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T]
147	    [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T] [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
148	    [Module A T] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
149	    (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
150	    ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0) (m' : ℕ) (_ : m' < d)
151	      (_ : Module (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
152	              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T))
153	      (_ : Module (Localization.Away g) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T))
154	      (_ : IsScalarTower (Localization.Away g)
155	              (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
156	              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)),
157	      Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
158	        (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)
159	-- can fix m' = d - 1 (single-variable elimination); strong induction accepts any m' < d.
160	```

162	And the **core's proof must be restructured** to generalize `A`:
163	```lean

165	∀ d, ∀ (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
166	       (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]

172	
173	## Recommendation
174	Pin `m := Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d) (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N)` as the generic
175	rank — idiomatic and `ℕ`-valued. Build `gf_generic_rank_ses` (no `g`) from `Fintype.linearCombination`

177	`LinearIndependent.restrict_scalars`), with `T := N ⧸ range φ` and `Module.IsTorsion P_d T`. Build
178	`gf_torsion_reindex` on the L4 existential template, using `Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors`
179	for `0 ≠ F ∈ Ann(T)` and the **project-side** Nagata single-variable elimination + denominator clearing
180	(shared with L4) to land over `MvPolynomial (Fin (d-1)) (Localization.Away g)`. **The load-bearing fix is
181	structural**: generalize the base domain `A` in the `Nat.strong_induction_on`, because the reindex changes
182	the base ring to `A_g` and the IH must be available there. None of these require weakening any pinned type.

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	\leanok
47	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
48	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
49	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
50	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
51	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
52	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
53	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
54	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
55	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
56	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
57	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
58	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
59	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
60	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
61	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
62	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
63	
64	  The formal target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic} regards \(M\) as an
65	  \(A\)-module through the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\), and packages the
66	  localisation \(M_f\) as the localised module at the powers of \(f\): there exists
67	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M}

98	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case as a thin Mathlib wrapper.}
99	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself (i.e.\ \(M\) is a finite
100	  \(A\)-module, not merely a finite \(B\)-module). Since \(A\) is noetherian, a finite
101	  \(A\)-module is finitely presented over \(A\). Let \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) be the
102	  localisation of \(A\) at the multiplicative set \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\). The
103	  generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M = S^{-1} M\) is a \emph{finite-dimensional}
104	  \(K\)-vector space, hence free over \(K\); thus the freeness hypothesis at the
105	  generic localisation is automatic, with no input from the structure of \(B\). The
106	  result is then exactly Mathlib's
107	  \texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers}
108	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Free}): a finitely-presented \(A\)-module
109	  that is free over the localisation \(A_S = K\) becomes free over
110	  \texttt{Localization.Away}~\(r\) after inverting some \(r \in S\), i.e.\ some
111	  \(r \in A\), \(r \ne 0\). Setting \(f := r\) gives \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). (The
112	  openness of the free locus is the \texttt{Module.freeLocus} API of
113	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Spectrum.Prime.FreeLocus}, which records that the locus
114	  where \(M\) is free over \(A\) is open and, by the above, non-empty.) This closes
115	  the theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
116	
117	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
118	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
119	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
120	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
121	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
122	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
123	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
124	  for exactly this residue.

127	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
128	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
129	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
130	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
131	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
132	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
133	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
134	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
135	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
136	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
137	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
138	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).

165	  axiom-clean. A finite module over a noetherian ring is finitely presented, so
166	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
167	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
168	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over

170	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
171	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take

221	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}

227	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
228	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)

275	
276	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
277	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)

279	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
280	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
281	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
282	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.

298	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).

1712	Let $A$ be a noetherian domain, 
1713	and $B$ a finite type $A$ algebra. Let $M$ be a finite $B$-module. 
1714	Then there exists an $f\in A$, $f\ne 0$, such that 
1715	the localisation $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$.
1716	\end{lemma}

1720	$B_K = K\otimes _AB$ is of finite type, and $M_K = K\otimes_AM$ is a 
1721	finite module over $B_K$. Let $n$ be the dimension of the support
1722	of $M_K$ over $\spec B_K$. We argue by induction on $n$, starting
1723	with $n=-1$ which is the case when $M_K =0$. In this case, as
1724	$K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$, each $v\in M$
1725	is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
1726	generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating 
1727	elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$.
1728	Hence $M_f =0$, proving the lemma when $n=-1$.
1729	
1730	Now let $n\ge 0$, and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
1731	As $B$ is noetherian and $M$ is assumed to be a finite $B$-module,
1732	there exists a finite filtration
1733	$$0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M$$
1734	where each $M_i$ is a $B$-submodule of $M$ such that for each $i\ge 1$
1735	the quotient module $M_i/M_{i-1}$ is isomorphic to $B/\pp_i$ for 
1736	some prime ideal $\pp_i$ in $B$. 
1737	
1738	Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact
1739	sequence of $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of
1740	$A$ such that $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over
1741	respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$, then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$
1742	where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact repeatedly.
1743	Therefore it is enough to prove the result when 
1744	$M$ is of the form $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. 
1745	This reduces us to the case where $B$ is a domain and $M=B$.

1748	$K\otimes_AB$ is non-zero), the map $A\to B$ must be injective.
1749	By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements

1751	such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra
1752	$K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and 
1753	the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent
1754	over $K$. (For simplicity of notation, we write $1\otimes b$ simply as $b$.)

1756	coefficients of equations of integral dependence

1758	algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$,

\begin{lemma}
[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
  \[
    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
  \]

  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
  \(\operatorname{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
  \(\operatorname{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
  \(Q = \operatorname{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.

  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.

  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[L5b — torsion reindex onto fewer variables]
  \label{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_reindex}
  % NOTE (shared engine): the single-variable Nagata change-of-variables together with
  % the denominator-clearing after inverting one element of A is the SAME engine the
  % Noether-normalisation step L4 (lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, via its sub-lemma
  % lem:gf_clear_one_denominator) requires. It should be built once in Lean and reused by
  % both: a general "make a nonzero F monic in the last variable and clear the leading
  % coefficient into one Localization.Away g" helper discharges this lemma and feeds L4.
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768).
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
  %   Write A_g := Localization.Away g and T_g := LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T.
  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
  %       (d : ℕ) (hd : 0 < d) (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T]
  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
  %       [Module A T] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0) (m' : ℕ) (_ : m' < d)
  %         (_ : Module (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
  %                 (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T))
  %         (_ : Module (Localization.Away g)
  %                 (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T))
  %         (_ : IsScalarTower (Localization.Away g)
  %                 (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
  %                 (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)),
  %         Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
  %           (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)
  %   one may always take m' = d - 1 (single-variable elimination); the strong induction
  %   accepts any m' < d.
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there
  exist a non-zero \(g \in A\) and an integer \(m' < d\) such that, after inverting \(g\),
  the localised module \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
  Since \(T\) is a finite torsion module over the noetherian domain \(P_d\), its
  annihilator meets the non-zero-divisors: there is
  \(0 \ne F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\) (a finite torsion module over a commutative
  ring has an annihilator containing a non-zero-divisor). Thus \(T\) is a finite module
  over the quotient \(P_d / (F)\).

  Apply a Nagata change of variables so that \(F\) becomes monic in the last variable
  \(X_d\) up to a non-zero leading coefficient \(g \in A\). After inverting \(g\), the
  image of \(F\) in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a unit multiple of a polynomial monic in
  \(X_d\); by the division algorithm \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_d] / (F)\) is module-finite over
  the subring \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}] = A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) with
  \(m' := d - 1\). Since \(T_g\) is finite over
  \(P_d[g^{-1}] / (F) = A_g[X_1, \dots, X_d] / (F)\), transitivity of module-finiteness
  shows \(T_g\) is finite over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). As \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using
  \(d \ge 1\)), this is the required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the
  number of variables dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
\end{proof}

  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
  % by Nat.strong_induction_on d, with A (and its CommRing/IsDomain/IsNoetherianRing
  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
  corresponding polynomial ring.

  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.

  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
      \(P_d\)-modules
      \[
        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
      \]
      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
      to \(A_g\) (see the structure note above).
    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Writing \(h = a / g^k\) with
      \(a \in A\), the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is isomorphic to \(A_{g a}\) and
      \((T_g)_h\) to \(T_{g a}\); thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over \((A_g)_h\) transports
      to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the localisation-tower free-transport
      fact \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}. Set \(f := g a \ne 0\); then
      \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
      completes the induction.
  \end{enumerate}
\end{proof}
